one_line_takeaway: A defense-free, DL-focused survey whose real deliverable is a two-axis taxonomy (7 attack characteristics x 6 algorithmic principles) plus the first structured map of data poisoning across the full LLM training-and-inference pipeline.
key_finding: The survey's synthesizing claims: (a) deep learning is distinctively vulnerable because over-parameterized networks can memorize poisoned/outlier samples without hurting clean accuracy, keeping malicious behavior dormant until triggered; (b) the LLM multi-stage pipeline multiplies attack surfaces relative to conventional supervised DL, and — per the primary works it cites — larger models are not inherently more robust and can generalize poisoned behavior more strongly; and (c) the field lacks standardized benchmarks and metrics, making cross-study comparison unreliable. Note these are the authors' distillations of cited results, not new measurements.
core_contribution: A survey (not an empirical paper) by Zhao et al. that consolidates the data-poisoning-attack literature specifically for deep learning, deliberately excluding defenses. Its own contributions are organizational, not new attacks: (1) a two-axis taxonomy of poisoning attacks — one axis over seven attack *characteristics* (objective, goal, attacker knowledge, stealthiness, scope, impact, variability) and one axis over six algorithmic *principles* (heuristic-based, label flipping, feature-space/feature-collision, bilevel optimization, influence-based, generative); and (2) what the authors claim is the first systematic treatment of data poisoning across the multi-stage LLM pipeline (pre-training, fine-tuning, preference alignment/RLHF, instruction tuning, prefix tuning, prompt tuning, in-context learning, and prompt injection). Table I positions the work against 11 prior surveys to argue those were broad (traditional ML + DL, attacks + defenses) while none covered the LLM-specific attack surface. It also ships a curated GitHub resource repository.
threat_model: The paper synthesizes, rather than proposes, a threat model: an adversary who injects or manipulates a fraction of training data during the training phase (as opposed to inference-time evasion) to either degrade overall accuracy (untargeted/availability), force misclassification of specific targets (targeted/integrity), or implant trigger-activated backdoors. Attacker knowledge is stratified into white-box (full model/params/data), grey-box (partial, e.g. architecture or a data subset), and black-box (query-only via a surrogate). Injection points span open-source datasets, web scraping, third-party vendors, federated/collaborative training, and — for LLMs — each training stage plus inference-time context (ICL, prompt injection).
limitations: As a survey it introduces no new method, dataset, or experiment, so all effectiveness numbers are inherited from cited primaries and are not independently verified here. It explicitly excludes defenses, so it offers no mitigation guidance. Coverage of quantitative comparability is acknowledged as weak (the paper itself calls for unified metrics). The text has noticeable copy-editing errors (garbled sentences, duplicated 'general framework' bullet lists, typos in author/model names in Table II), which slightly undercuts rigor. The 'first survey on LLM data poisoning' novelty claim is self-asserted.

datasets_used: None used by this survey itself (no experiments). CIFAR-10 appears only as the benchmark in a cited primary (Jha et al., FLIP); MNIST/handwritten-digit, traffic-sign, and face-recognition datasets appear only in Table II as the application domains of cited attacks.
quantitative_results: Convex Polytope Attack (cited, Zhu et al. 2019): ~50% attack success rate while poisoning only 1% of training data in black-box settings
FLIP (cited, Jha et al. 2023): corrupting just 2% of labels on CIFAR-10 achieves near-perfect backdoor success (label-only attack)
POISONPROMPT (cited, ref [159]): >90% attack success rate across 6 datasets and 3 LLMs while preserving clean-task performance
Instruction-tuning attack (cited, Wan et al.): as few as 100 poisoned samples embed a hidden trigger
ICLPoison (cited, He et al.): up to a 10% drop in in-context-learning accuracy, including on GPT-4
Multimodal fine-tuning (cited, Ibanez-Lissen et al.): poisoning rate as low as 5% yields notable cross-modality attack effectiveness
Prefix-tuning attack (cited, ref [156]): poisoning ratios of 1-10% on T5-small and GPT-2 introduce persistent adversarial behavior evading standard filters
Survey scope: Table I compares against 11 prior surveys; ~39 cited works; taxonomy = 7 attack-characteristic dimensions x 6 algorithmic families
baselines_compared: N/A — no method is evaluated by the authors. The only comparison is qualitative: Table I contrasts this survey's coverage against 11 existing surveys (Pitropakis 2019, Tahmasebian 2020, Ahmed 2021, Ramirez 2022, Fan 2022, Tian 2022, Wang 2022, Goldblum 2022, Xia 2023, Tayyab 2023, Cina 2023, Cina 2024) along axes such as TML/DL scope, attacks/defenses, and LLM coverage.
